So, at a high level, what Pieces is, so we're building what we call a long-term memory and essentially, you know, you've obviously, you know, I'm sure you probably use a lot of these tools as you should since you're at a company that's all about programmatic SEO.
If you think like ChatGPT or Claude or any of these tools, one thing that they lack right now.
Is memory.
Of course, ChatGPT just announced like the memory component, but that's limited to your chats.
What we were building is actually like the underlying platform.
So it's like a desktop application that's on your machine and it captures everything that you're doing.
So if you're in your browser, you're, you know, looking at a file, you're using some application, we capture your screen.
So and then we turn that into context on the back end as a vector database.
And essentially, you can then ask questions through the application.
Of course, we support like all the LLMs, like OpenAI and Enthropic and all these.
And you can ask questions and the responses will be tailored to your specific workflow.
And, you know, for example, like if you ask, hey, who was I just talking to, you know, yesterday from Boston?
Chances are, I will probably record it from your calendar and say, hey, you were talking to Smit Patel from Pieces who lives in Boston, right?
So much more tailored versus like obviously like any other AI tool right now.
I joined to basically revamp the entire growth, positioning, obviously all that stuff.
And one of the key areas that the company always had when I joined was SEO.

And when I say it had, I use the word loosely because the person who was running it previously, who no longer here, basically went and hired a bunch of, you know, low quality writers and had a  ton of, lack of better words, a  ton of content that was written, which was .
And, you know, when I came in, you know, the person was super excited about all this traffic we were getting, but I knew very well that the traffic was bad quality.

And, you know, I think the best way I think about it is like, I can write an article about like 20 dad jokes and get a bunch of visitors to it, but those people are not going to buy my developer tool, right?
So it's like not knowing your audience and writing, you know, content that gets a bunch of impressions, not necessarily the same thing.

So we've been kind of going on this journey, trying to, you know, revamp the content strategy and focus more on quality, not work on work with a lot of external writers that were, you know, probably just, you know, using AI to generate some of that stuff or just writing garbage.
And actually shifted a lot of that to in-house.
So we shifted to some of our engineers writing content, some of our dev world folks writing content.

So that's where we are right now.

There's just a lot that goes into that, to really get that engine humming, but when you can, the results can be quite amazing, and what really, you know, started to be the...

The stage where GrowthX started to form was when Marcel started to share a bit of how he was doing this at DeepGram where he was CMO, basically taking some of those same learnings from developing those content engines from a typical human-led approach, but how can we create workflows that are really purpose-built to address all those jobs to be done of folks in those roles?

And it's not just about automating the way of the work they're doing, but it's about helping arm them, helping supercharge them in a way that helps them to spend more time on the things that are going to drive impact, the things that are going to be more around strategy, thinking what comes next, et cetera, and really just helping enrich their work in a variety of different ways.

lead investor for DeepGram is actually our lead investor at our Series A.

move into is our article calibration phase.

I'm sure I'm going to sound biased in my own take here, but when I go back to why I signed up for GrowthX in the beginning, and that perspective still holds true of my hypothesis back then, there's not going to be a day where Google just programmatically says, was this phrase created by AI?
Yes, penalty.
No, you're fine.
Like, at the end of the day, this is a lot of things around all the algorithm changes.
over the years have been overcomplicated.
What Google is trying to do as much as possible is create a good user experience behind that search.
You have a search, you have a question, I want to serve you the best results for that question.
Before you got there, it doesn't matter to Google.
It is more that this is rich content that is unique to you that is engaging and answering that question.
So everything we build is trying to do that still today.
The HubSpots of the world and those that have been making into LinkedIn, so many hot takes about this.
What is a little annoying to me at times when I see that is people are missing some of the important nuance and context of what actually happened with those changes.
To tell a short story here, one of my big bets last year when I was at Homebase was seeing some traction in affiliates.
I'm going to double down on affiliates.
Unfortunately, that didn't end up working out because affiliates got massively dinged by algorithmic changes last year.
And what happened last year was Google was trying to...
Better understand what is your website about?
Like, what is the world in which you operate within that I'm going to say you are an authority in this space?
But those affiliate sites were very, very broad.
were trying to cover everything under the sun, generate traffic over any topic they could possibly do so they could run ads over it.

The HubSpot to the world, what they were doing is they were going after some of those really, really broad topics to give them authority to be able to rank for some of the things they really, really cared about.
Back when I was at Trey, used to study Zapier's program a lot.
They did a bit of this.
One of the things they went after that always stands out to me is, like, how to address business casual.
They had all this content about, I like, what does this have to do with integration automation?
But, like, it was really easy to rank for, and that gave them a lot of, like, oomph to put behind ranking for, like, that demand capture.
Like, I want to integrate Salesforce to Marketo and things of that nature.
But what Google has started to say over the years is that that kind of strategy is not going work anymore.

and just creating amazing content for them.
